informal: Show that if $a$ is negative then $p \equiv q(4 a)$ together with $p\not | a$ imply $(a / p)=(a / q)$.
reasoning: The Lean signature captures the same claim: a < 0, p ≡ q mod 4a, p ∤ a, conclude legendreSym p a = legendreSym q a. The apparent absence of an "odd prime" hypothesis is harmless because p ≡ q mod 4a with |4a| ≥ 4 forces p = 2 ↔ q = 2, in which case the conclusion is trivial; legendreSym is well-defined in Mathlib for p = 2 as well. The seemingly missing q ∤ a hypothesis follows from the given ones: if q | a, then since a | 4a we get a | p, forcing a ∈ {±1,±p}, contradicting either a < 0 with q prime or p ∤ a. The modular condition `[ZMOD 4*a]` is the correct integer congruence mod 4a, and types/coercions match. So the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal one.
